speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Tape three, [? Leslie ?] Primack. Can you tell me, have you ever returned to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 19-- about a year ago, 1995, a group of my colleagues from AJEX, the Association of Jewish Ex-Servicemen and Women, we did a day trip. We went back to Belsen to commemorate and to pay our respects and to relive. Not everybody in the party were liberators. But there were members of families of the victims, and there were two ladies in our group who were actually prisoners there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry. But I can-- I can get them for you at a late-- at a later stage. We were met by two British Army officers who escorted us to the camp. The camp has become completely sanitized. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the latter part of June '45, the entire camp was burnt to the ground by the authorities, not so much to destroy the camp, but to control the disease that was running rife, typhus. And all-- all there is now there is a building at the gate to the camp, which is now a museum which contains photographs of what was then at the time. We walked into the open ground, and there were mass graves, huge mountains of mass graves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 5,000 in this one, 2,000 in that one. And on the left-hand side is a cross, a monument, a cross to those who were not Jewish there. And way in the far ground was a memorial, a huge memorial, 15 feet high, about 20 or 30 feet wide, with names of the survivors. And we had arranged to conduct a service. We laid wreaths. And while we were there, there were some visiting groups of school children, accompanied by nuns, who were mesmerized by who we were. There were about 60 or 70 of us. We had our banners, our flags. And they became very interested in who we were and what we were. And we talked to quite a lot of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The two nuns, in particular, spoke excellent English. In  fact, many of the children spoke good English. And they were very inquisitive as to us, asked  us many questions. We tried to reply as best we could. In fact, we have some pictures taken of the visit. The two Army officers were with us all the way, and they showed us extreme courtesy. The local authorities, the civilian authorities, extended every facility that we wanted. And I can't say that we were pleased that we went, that we were glad that we went. And on our return journey, we stopped off at one of the military cemeteries that were in the area, and we paid our respects to not only to all foreign soldiers, but to, in particular, there were some Jewish graves in the area, and we had a short service there. But the camp today can best be described as a park. Unlike the other concentration camps, where there is evidence of ovens and other buildings, there's nothing there at Belsen today. In fact, if one didn't know what went on there, one would never really understand. So we have to resort to the museum and the pictorial evidence. And we spent the day there, and we were glad that we went, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say there's a memorial to the survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a memorial to the dead? Is there a list of names of the dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. I'm sorry. I should have said the dead. I beg your pardon. Yeah, yeah, should have said the dead, yes. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your attitude towards the German people of your generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That question has been asked of me countless times. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We can't live in the past. But on the other hand, we must remember the past. I know the majority of the Germans today are the sons, daughters, husbands of the German people who lived at that time and who fought at that time. And I have no personal animosity towards them as individuals. But one cannot forget what Germany did as a nation. I know that they have made attempts to recompense, to make restitution, goes to Israel and other organizations, which is quite right. But history can never be eradicated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever told your son what you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever told your son what you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, and my grandsons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they react?

speaker: interviewee
text: They accepted what I told them. I don't think it really penetrated. I don't think they could appreciate the enormity of what took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you kept in touch with anybody you served with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: --during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, indeed, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But time, time has taken its toll.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We used to have reunions, particularly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] particularly with the Dunkirk Veterans Association, which now is getting on. There are very few of us left now. But I do try. I do maintain with other servicemen and women through the Army, the Navy, or the Air Force, purely as ex-servicemen and women of the Jewish faith.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say to those who deny the occurrence of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they say they deny it, then I think I have to say very little to them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There has been so much on record-- in films, newspapers, books. And if they still deny that it took place, I think any personal discussion between myself would be a waste of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to say to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And do not forget to remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We say it should never happen again, but history is a funny thing. Never take anything for granted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jews, as a nation, have been persecuted and destroyed since the beginning of time, but we're still here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as long as people remember and continue to remember, we will survive as a nation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like  to add anything else before we finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's about it, I think. I think I've taxed my memory considerably. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's only a fragment, I'm sure, of my five and a half years. I'm sure there are other things that were there. But sitting here today, I remembered-- I'm trying to remember the more important things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photo, [? Leslie? ?] [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a school photograph. It's the school I went to-- Eleanor Road School in Hackney, East London. I was about 10. So I guess that was taken about 1928. There were about 40 to 45 pupils in the school. It was an elementary school, just teaching basic subjects. There's nothing remarkable about it. There were hundreds of schools in the country like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give some indication of where you are in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'm in fourth row back in the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose I'm the only one that can recognize myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who this is a photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of myself taken at Catterick Camp, Yorkshire during my training days in the autumn of 1939. And I-- I was trained as a dispatch rider. The L on the front is not for [? Leslie, ?] it's just learner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell us who the young couple are in this photo? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my late wife and myself. We were married on the 21st of July 1940. I was 21, and she was 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Rose, although her nickname was Brownie. And we were married at the Devonshire Road synagogue, which is in East London, on the 21st of July 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a picture of myself [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] taken in Germany, perhaps during 1945, and a Jeep. I had disposed of my motorcycle by then, and I was now driving a Jeep, which, at one time, was my home. I would sleep and eat in it for many days on end, depending on which particular part of the war we were dealing with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a group of four Jewish ex-servicemen-- Jewish ex-servicemen who were part of a magnificent gesture by the London Taxi Drivers Association, who provided about 70 taxis and 200 ex-servicemen and women, interdenominational, and they took us back to Normandy on a visit. Myself is there. The man to my immediate left is [? Jeffrey ?] [? Stuart. ?] But I'm sorry to say I don't know the names of the other two, except that we were, the four of us, were Jewish. But there were other non-Jewish personnel in the group. The taxi drivers formed up in a-- on Horse Guards Parade, which is in the center of London. And in convoy with a police escort, we drove down to Portsmouth, which was the main embarkation port for the D-Day landings. And we went across on a very luxurious ship, somewhat different to the ships that we crossed in 50 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We arrived at a port in Normandy. We were met by the local mayor and a police escort. We were taken to three different hotels, where we were to spend the night. And the following two days, we were treated with the highest attention and detail. We visited local municipal buildings. We were met by civic dignitaries. We were presented a medal commemorating the invasion of Normandy. We went to a civic banquet. We visited British cemeteries and paid our respects in the area. And we also conducted services, both for Jew and non-Jew. It was a memorable three days. And our thanks to all that took part, with thanks to the London Taxi Drivers Association. And you can see one of the taxis in the right-hand corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the date of  that photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in June 19-- 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this photo? [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: As I referred earlier in this interview, 60 or so of us went back to Belsen on a day's visit. This photograph was taken just immediately in front of the memorial where we laid our wreaths. The lady on the extreme right is a survivor of Belsen. Her name is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Is that on the left as we're looking at it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Extreme right, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Extreme left as we're looking at it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looking at the picture, the lady on the extreme left is Gena Turgel, who was a prisoner and a survivor of Belsen. The man next to her left is her late husband Norman Turgel. They met and married in the camp, and the service was conducted by Reverend Hardman. The next gentleman in the center is [? Alan ?] [? Conway. ?] He is chairman [BACKROUND NOISE] of the Edgware branch of the Association of Jewish Ex-Servicemen and Women. The dignified gentleman with the beard is Robert Goldberg. He is the public relations officer of the Middlesex branch of the Normandy Veterans Association. And the gentleman to his left is myself. This picture was taken in 1995, June 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This picture is a group of Jewish ex-servicemen who, on the trip with the taxi drivers group, we visited a cemetery in northern France in Bayeux. We conducted a service [BACKROUND NOISE] in front of two headstones, both Jewish servicemen, who had paid the supreme sacrifice. The reverend gentleman in the center, believe it or not, is non-Jewish. He happened to be at the cemetery at the time and asked whether he would be allowed to join us, and we agreed. And he joined us in the service, which was a Jewish service, a memorial service, to the dead. I'm not in this picture, but all of them are colleagues of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Would you like to tell us about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Believe it or not, this is myself, not current figure. I was at a friend's house recently, and I saw this motorcycle in the back. And I just couldn't resist getting on it, considering how much time I'd spent on a motorcycle during my wartime days. I doubt very much whether I can drive it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the significance for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a copy of a newspaper, which was printed on the 7th of June, 1994. Please note the price of one penny.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- '44, yes. And picture tells a-- a story. The tanks were 10 miles in. And the whole of that newspaper dealt with the successes that the Allied troops were having invading Normandy and the liberation of-- of Europe as a whole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell me about this certificate?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a certificate that says, from the City of Dunkirk, presented to myself, Morris [? Leslie ?] Primack, of the Royal Corps Signals in memory of the-- of the combat of the war, which took place in May and June of 1940. It is signed by four of the dignitaries of the city of Dunkirk. It was presented to me as I took part in the defense and the activities that took place during that time. [BACKROUND NOISE] And I was also presented with a medal in addition to the certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the-- Ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, can you tell us about the medals?

speaker: interviewee
text: The medals really are one of-- to commemorate each particular campaign. It was not presented for any particular act of bravery. They just represented the particular theater of war that I happened to be serving in at that time. The one on the extreme left is known as the 1939-'45 Star. The next one is the France and Germany Star. The next one is [BACKROUND NOISE] the North Africa Star. Then came the Defense Medal and then the Victory Medal. In the lower, the one on the left was presented by the ministers of Normandy. It's really a civilian award to commemorate the landings in Normandy in 1944. The yellow one and in the center has been presented together with the certificate that I just showed you, and that is known as the Dunkirk Medal, being a survivor. And the next one is the medal commemorating the liberation of France. Both of the bottom three were presented by the French government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Leslie, would you like to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son and heir.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lack of hair, but heir, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Known as Geoffrey David Primack, but known as Geoffrey Primack. I've known him since the day he was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and he's been a total source of comfort to me all through the years. As a youngster, he might have been a bit troublesome. But in the latter years, and since I lost my partner and he lost his mother, he has been a total source of comfort for me. And I promised not to be too much trouble to him. This youngster on my right is my number two grandson Matthew. I have an older grandson, who is away working at the moment. His name is Daniel. And these two are the pride and my joy, and I'm very grateful to have them. And they're a source of great comfort to me. My daughter-in-law Sandra, unfortunately, is keeping the family in a manner which they are unaccustomed to. She's working. And I would have liked to have her being here, but unfortunately, she-- she can't be there. But that's my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeffrey, I wonder whether you would like to say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I'm delighted to hear what Dad said. It doesn't come as a surprise, but lovely to hear  it. And I was born in 1946, obviously a year after the war had ended. So Dad obviously came home from his-- his survival in some very difficult times and decided better times were ahead. I was born in '46, obviously knowing nothing of the war. What I have heard and seen since that time clearly makes me think I hope never to have to go into anything like that or be a part of anything like that and neither my sons after me. And we pray that that will never happen. We've heard some of the stories from Dad over the years of what took place, some of the narrow escapes. One particular story of how he was very nearly killed, and it was only by, I suppose, luck. There was a dispatch rider went out on his bike when he was unable to do so, and that rider was indeed killed. And Dad has said that [INAUDIBLE] was called elsewhere, but could easily have been on that bike and not been here today, and we would not be here today. And the rest of the story takes its own course  from that moment. Dad has suffered over the years, I'm sure, from memories. He believes during  the war that certain things did happen to him that has affected his health since that time. And one will never know if that is true, but it is most likely to be true. And it is a great shame that that has indeed limited his ability to enjoy a full and active life at this time. But he is here. He has survived. Many others didn't. And we are, to say the least, very pleased he did survive, and we're very pleased that he's here now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure, sir.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to add anything, [? Leslie? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm touched by what he said, but it's not totally unexpected. We are very close to each other. We're more like brothers rather than a father and son. And for that, I'm very grateful. And long may it reign.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Can you tell us about  this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the spring of 1945 and to celebrate the festival of Passover, a seder night service was held in the restaurant of a department store in Brussels. It was a Bon Marche, and the owners were Jewish. Their name was [? Vaxelaire. ?] I'm sitted-- I'm seated in the center front. And by coincidence, the man on my right is my-- my cousin, also [? Leslie, ?] [? Leslie ?] [? Gilson. ?] We met by accident. I knew that he was somewhere in Europe. And by sheer coincidence, we met that evening, and we're actually sitting next to each other. It was a wonderful evening. There were perhaps some 500 servicemen and women of the Allied forces.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
